edge: Learned optimizers are a type of neural networks trained to act as optimizers.
Learned optimizers have the potential to accelerate the training of machine learning models.
Blackbox learned optimizers often struggle with stability when applied to new tasks.
The paper investigates inductive biases and stability properties of optimization algorithms.
The noisy quadratic model is used to characterize conditions for stable optimization.
Modifications to both architecture and meta-training can improve stability and inductive bias.
The improved learned optimizer outperforms the state of the art on neural network training tasks.
The improved optimizer shows better meta-training speed and ability to generalize to different tasks.
Algorithms for stochastic non-convex optimization are fundamental to modern neural networks.
These algorithms are fundamental for training modern neural networks.
Choice of both optimization algorithms and hyperparameters affects model performance and stability.

Optimization algorithms are usually chosen based on analysis in the convex setting or informal strategies.

Inductive biases are integrated in few-shot learning through various algorithmic primitives.
Recurrent networks are an example of blackbox models used in early approaches to few-shot learning.
Gradient descent is one of the algorithmic inductive biases exploited for few-shot learning.
Convex optimization is another inductive bias used in the context of few-shot learning.
Bayesian inference serves as an inductive bias for solving few-shot learning tasks.
The difficulty of stable training of large models led to the development of adaptive optimization algorithms.
Adagrad is an example of an adaptive optimization algorithm designed for invariant hyperparameter choice.

Adam is another well-known adaptive optimization algorithm.

A neural network can be meta-learned to choose hyperparameters based on training history.
Meta-learning hyperparameters results in improved stability of learning algorithms.
